title: RPYR-0003 — `Future.__await__().throw(E)` aborts when `E.__new__` returns a non-exception: `downcast().unwrap()` on the `.call()` result (`_asyncio.rs:1081`)
one_line_repro: f.__await__().throw(E)  where E(Exception).__new__ returns a non-exception
cpython_behavior: raises TypeError (a __new__ returning a non-exception is rejected before it becomes the raised value)
guarded_twin: crates/stdlib/src/_asyncio.rs — the map_err downcast idiom used correctly at 879, 920, 2038, 2297, 2762; this site .unwrap()s instead
notes: TOOLKIT META-EVAL HIGHLIGHT: the panic-site *scanner* flagged this FIX, but the panic-site *agent* WRONGLY dismissed it as ACCEPTABLE via a faulty payload-invariant argument (it assumed exc_type.call(()) returns a BaseException instance because exc_type was fast_issubclass(BaseException)-gated). The git-history-analyzer disagreed, and an empirical binary test settled it: `class E(Exception): __new__ returns 42` makes `E()` return 42, so the downcast of the .call() result fails. The gate is on exc_type (the class); the downcast is on exc (the instance) — a different value. This is the case that motivated keeping the invariant-downcast calibration's 'same-variable gate' rule (a gate on a DIFFERENT variable does not down-rank).
